name: P/E Ratio
aliases: price to earnings ratio, pe ratio, price-to-earnings
definition: The P/E ratio measures how much investors pay for each dollar of a company's earnings. Formula: Stock Price ÷ Earnings Per Share.
keyTakeaway: High P/E (>25) may signal growth expectations; low P/E (<15) may indicate undervaluation or investor concerns.

name: Market Capitalization
aliases: market cap, mcap
definition: Total value of a company's outstanding shares. Formula: Stock Price × Total Shares Outstanding.
keyTakeaway: Large cap (>$10B) = more stable; mid cap ($2–10B); small cap (<$2B) = higher growth potential but more volatile.

name: Dividends
aliases: dividend, dividend yield, dividend payout
definition: A portion of company profits distributed to shareholders, typically quarterly. Dividend Yield = Annual Dividend ÷ Stock Price.
keyTakeaway: Dividends provide passive income. Growth companies often reinvest instead of paying dividends.

name: ETF
aliases: exchange traded fund, exchange-traded fund, index fund
definition: A basket of securities (stocks, bonds, commodities) that trades on an exchange like a stock. Provides instant diversification.
keyTakeaway: ETFs offer diversification, low fees, and trading flexibility compared to mutual funds.

name: Earnings Per Share
aliases: eps, earnings-per-share
definition: Net income divided by total outstanding shares. Shows how much profit is attributable to each share of stock.
keyTakeaway: Rising EPS generally signals improving profitability. Compare EPS growth rate across peers.

name: Dollar Cost Averaging
aliases: dca, dollar-cost averaging
definition: Investing a fixed amount at regular intervals regardless of price. Reduces impact of volatility on overall purchase cost.
keyTakeaway: DCA removes the need to time the market. You buy more shares when prices are low and fewer when prices are high.

name: Beta
aliases: stock beta, beta coefficient
definition: Measures a stock's volatility relative to the overall market. Beta of 1.0 = moves with the market; >1.0 = more volatile.
keyTakeaway: High beta stocks amplify market movements (both gains and losses). Conservative investors may prefer low-beta stocks.

name: Moving Average
aliases: ma, sma, simple moving average, ema, exponential moving average
definition: Average price over a set period (e.g. 50-day or 200-day). Smooths out short-term fluctuations to show trends.
keyTakeaway: Price crossing above the 200-day MA is bullish ('golden cross'); crossing below is bearish ('death cross').

name: RSI
aliases: relative strength index, rsi indicator
definition: Momentum oscillator measuring speed and magnitude of price changes. Ranges from 0 to 100.
keyTakeaway: RSI above 70 = overbought (may pull back); RSI below 30 = oversold (may bounce).

name: Diversification
aliases: portfolio diversification, asset allocation
definition: Spreading investments across different asset classes, sectors, and geographies to reduce risk.
keyTakeaway: Don't put all eggs in one basket. A diversified portfolio reduces the impact of any single investment's poor performance.

name: Short Selling
aliases: shorting, short sale, going short
definition: Borrowing shares to sell them, hoping to buy them back later at a lower price. Profits from declining stock prices.
keyTakeaway: Short selling has unlimited loss potential since a stock can rise indefinitely. It's an advanced strategy.

name: Compound Interest
aliases: compounding, compound growth, compound returns
definition: Earning returns on both your original investment and previously earned returns. Often called the 'eighth wonder of the world'.
keyTakeaway: The longer you invest, the more powerful compounding becomes. Starting early matters more than investing large amounts later.

name: Volatility
aliases: vol, implied volatility, historical volatility, vix
definition: Statistical measure of how much a security's price fluctuates. Higher volatility = larger price swings.
keyTakeaway: Volatility is not the same as risk, but it measures uncertainty. The VIX (fear index) tracks S&P 500 expected volatility.

name: Bull Market
aliases: bull run, bullish, bull trend
definition: A market condition where prices are rising or expected to rise. Generally defined as a 20%+ rise from recent lows.
keyTakeaway: Bull markets are driven by economic growth, strong earnings, and investor optimism. They don't last forever.

name: Bear Market
aliases: bear trend, bearish, market crash
definition: A market condition where prices decline 20%+ from recent highs. Often accompanied by pessimism and fear.
keyTakeaway: Bear markets are painful but historically temporary. They often present buying opportunities for long-term investors.

name: Margin Trading
aliases: buying on margin, margin account, leverage
definition: Borrowing money from a broker to buy securities. Amplifies both gains and losses.
keyTakeaway: Margin can magnify returns but also magnify losses. A margin call forces you to add funds or sell positions.

name: Bonds
aliases: bond, fixed income, treasury, corporate bond
definition: Debt instruments where you lend money to a government or corporation in exchange for periodic interest and return of principal.
keyTakeaway: Bonds are generally lower risk than stocks. Bond prices move inversely to interest rates.

name: Options
aliases: call option, put option, options trading, derivatives
definition: Contracts giving the right (not obligation) to buy (call) or sell (put) an asset at a set price before a deadline.
keyTakeaway: Options offer leverage and hedging but are complex. Most options expire worthless — understand the risks.

name: Revenue
aliases: sales, top line, total revenue, gross revenue
definition: Total income from goods sold or services provided before any expenses are deducted. Also called the 'top line'.
keyTakeaway: Revenue growth signals business expansion. Compare revenue growth rate to peers and industry averages.

name: EBITDA
aliases: ebitda margin, earnings before interest taxes depreciation amortization
definition: Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization. Measures core operating profitability.
keyTakeaway: EBITDA removes financing and accounting effects, making it useful for comparing companies across industries.

name: 401(k)
aliases: 401k, traditional 401k, employer 401k
definition: An employer-sponsored retirement savings plan allowing employees to contribute pre-tax income. 2025 limit: $23,500 ($30,500 if 50+).
keyTakeaway: Always contribute enough to get the full employer match — it's free money. Contributions reduce taxable income.

name: Roth IRA
aliases: roth, roth individual retirement account, backdoor roth
definition: A retirement account funded with after-tax dollars. Qualified withdrawals in retirement are completely tax-free. 2025 limit: $7,000 ($8,000 if 50+).
keyTakeaway: Best for those who expect higher taxes in retirement. Income limits apply but can be bypassed via 'backdoor Roth' conversion.

name: Traditional IRA
aliases: ira, individual retirement account
definition: A retirement account where contributions may be tax-deductible and investments grow tax-deferred until withdrawal.
keyTakeaway: Deductibility depends on income and employer plan availability. Required Minimum Distributions start at age 73.

name: Required Minimum Distribution
aliases: rmd, minimum distribution, required distribution
definition: The minimum amount you must withdraw annually from tax-deferred retirement accounts starting at age 73 (as of SECURE 2.0 Act).
keyTakeaway: Failing to take RMDs results in a 25% penalty on the amount not withdrawn. Roth IRAs have no RMDs for the original owner.

name: Roth Conversion
aliases: roth conversion ladder, convert to roth, backdoor roth conversion
definition: Moving money from a Traditional IRA or 401(k) to a Roth IRA, paying taxes on the converted amount now for tax-free growth later.
keyTakeaway: Best done in low-income years. The 'Roth conversion ladder' is a strategy for early retirees to access funds penalty-free.

name: HSA
aliases: health savings account, hsa account, triple tax advantage
definition: A tax-advantaged account for medical expenses available with high-deductible health plans. 2025 limits: $4,150 individual, $8,300 family.
keyTakeaway: Triple tax advantage: tax-deductible contributions, tax-free growth, tax-free withdrawals for medical expenses. After 65, works like a Traditional IRA.

name: Social Security
aliases: social security benefits, ssa, social security retirement
definition: Government retirement benefits based on your highest 35 years of earnings. Full retirement age is 67 for those born after 1960.
keyTakeaway: Delaying benefits past full retirement age increases payments by 8% per year until 70. Claiming at 62 reduces benefits by ~30%.

name: Catch-Up Contributions
aliases: catch up, over 50 contributions, additional contributions
definition: Additional amounts people 50+ can contribute to retirement accounts above standard limits. 401k: $7,500 extra; IRA: $1,000 extra.
keyTakeaway: Take advantage of catch-up provisions starting at 50 to accelerate retirement savings in your highest-earning years.

name: 529 Plan
aliases: 529, college savings plan, education savings
definition: A tax-advantaged savings plan for education expenses. Contributions grow tax-free and withdrawals for qualified expenses are tax-free.
keyTakeaway: Many states offer a tax deduction for 529 contributions. Unused funds can be rolled to Roth IRA (up to $35K lifetime) under SECURE 2.0.

name: Coverdell ESA
aliases: coverdell, education savings account, esa
definition: An education savings account with a $2,000/year contribution limit. Can be used for K-12 and college expenses.
keyTakeaway: More flexible than 529 plans for K-12 expenses but has income limits and lower contribution caps.

name: Tax-Loss Harvesting
aliases: tax loss, harvesting losses, offset gains
definition: Selling investments at a loss to offset capital gains taxes. Up to $3,000 in net losses can offset ordinary income annually.
keyTakeaway: Beware the wash sale rule: you can't repurchase a 'substantially identical' security within 30 days. Consider buying a similar ETF instead.

name: Capital Gains Tax
aliases: capital gains, long term capital gains, short term capital gains, ltcg, stcg
definition: Tax on profit from selling an asset. Short-term (held <1 year): taxed as ordinary income. Long-term (held >1 year): lower rates (0%, 15%, or 20%).
keyTakeaway: Hold investments >1 year to qualify for lower long-term rates. The 0% rate applies to income under ~$44K single / $89K married.

name: Wash Sale Rule
aliases: wash sale, 30 day rule, wash sale violation
definition: IRS rule that disallows a tax loss deduction if you buy a substantially identical security within 30 days before or after the sale.
keyTakeaway: To avoid wash sales, wait 31 days or buy a different but similar investment (e.g., sell SPY, buy VOO).

name: Alternative Minimum Tax
aliases: amt, minimum tax, alternative tax
definition: A parallel tax system that limits certain deductions and credits. Designed to ensure high-income earners pay a minimum level of tax.
keyTakeaway: ISO stock option exercises, large state tax deductions, and certain municipal bonds can trigger AMT. Plan with a tax advisor.

name: SALT Deduction
aliases: state and local tax, salt cap, salt deduction limit
definition: Deduction for state and local taxes paid. Currently capped at $10,000 per household under the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act.
keyTakeaway: The $10K SALT cap particularly affects residents of high-tax states like CA, NY, NJ. May expire after 2025.

name: Standard Deduction
aliases: standard deduction vs itemized, personal deduction
definition: A fixed amount that reduces your taxable income. 2025: $15,000 single, $30,000 married filing jointly.
keyTakeaway: Only itemize if your deductions (mortgage interest, charity, SALT) exceed the standard deduction. About 90% of filers use the standard.

name: Tax Bracket
aliases: marginal tax rate, income tax bracket, federal tax bracket
definition: The percentage rate at which your last dollar of income is taxed. US has 7 brackets: 10%, 12%, 22%, 24%, 32%, 35%, 37%.
keyTakeaway: Only income WITHIN each bracket is taxed at that rate (marginal), not your entire income. Effective rate is always lower than marginal rate.

name: Qualified Dividend
aliases: qualified dividends, ordinary dividend, dividend tax rate
definition: Dividends from US or qualifying foreign corporations held for 60+ days. Taxed at lower long-term capital gains rates instead of ordinary income rates.
keyTakeaway: Qualified dividends can be taxed at 0%, 15%, or 20% depending on income — much lower than ordinary income rates.

name: Mutual Fund
aliases: mutual funds, open-end fund, actively managed fund
definition: A pooled investment vehicle managed by a professional fund manager. Priced once daily at market close (NAV).
keyTakeaway: Mutual funds offer professional management but typically charge higher expense ratios (0.5-1.5%) than ETFs (0.03-0.2%).

name: Index Fund
aliases: index investing, passive investing, total market fund
definition: A fund that tracks a market index (like S&P 500) rather than trying to beat it. Features very low fees.
keyTakeaway: Over 15 years, 90%+ of actively managed funds underperform the S&P 500 index. Low fees compound massively over time.

name: REIT
aliases: real estate investment trust, reit fund, real estate fund
definition: A company that owns, operates, or finances income-producing real estate. Must distribute 90%+ of taxable income as dividends.
keyTakeaway: REITs offer real estate exposure without owning property. They typically yield 3-6% but may be less tax-efficient than other investments.

name: Futures Contract
aliases: futures, commodity futures, futures trading
definition: A standardized agreement to buy or sell a specific commodity or financial instrument at a set price on a future date.
keyTakeaway: Futures provide leverage and hedging for commodities, currencies, and indices. They require margin and have unlimited loss potential.

name: Cryptocurrency
aliases: crypto, digital currency, bitcoin, ethereum, blockchain
definition: Digital assets that use cryptography and blockchain technology. Bitcoin and Ethereum are the two largest by market cap.
keyTakeaway: Crypto is highly volatile (50%+ drawdowns common). Consider limiting to 1-5% of portfolio. Taxed as property by the IRS.

name: Stablecoin
aliases: usdc, usdt, tether, dai, stablecoins
definition: Cryptocurrencies pegged to a stable asset (usually USD). Used for trading, yield generation, and payment settlement.
keyTakeaway: Not all stablecoins are equally safe. USDC (Circle) is fully reserved; algorithmic stablecoins (like UST) have failed catastrophically.

name: Sharpe Ratio
aliases: sharpe, risk adjusted return, sharpe ratio formula
definition: Measures risk-adjusted return: (Portfolio Return - Risk-Free Rate) ÷ Portfolio Standard Deviation.
keyTakeaway: Sharpe > 1.0 is good, > 2.0 is very good, > 3.0 is excellent. The higher the Sharpe, the better the risk-adjusted performance.

name: Alpha
aliases: jensen's alpha, excess return, alpha generation
definition: The excess return of an investment relative to a benchmark index. Positive alpha means outperformance.
keyTakeaway: True alpha is rare and hard to sustain. Most 'alpha' disappears after accounting for fees, risk, and luck.

name: Standard Deviation
aliases: std dev, standard deviation finance, volatility measure
definition: Measures the dispersion of returns around the average. Higher standard deviation = more variability in returns.
keyTakeaway: Stocks typically have 15-20% annual std dev; bonds 4-8%. Use it to understand how much your portfolio value might swing.

name: Value at Risk
aliases: var, value-at-risk, portfolio var
definition: The maximum expected loss over a given time period at a given confidence level. Example: 95% daily VaR of $10K means you expect to lose no more than $10K on 95% of days.
keyTakeaway: VaR is useful for setting risk limits but underestimates tail risk (black swan events). Use alongside stress testing.

name: Correlation
aliases: asset correlation, correlation coefficient, diversification benefit
definition: Measures how two assets move relative to each other. +1 = perfect positive (move together); -1 = perfect negative (move opposite); 0 = no relationship.
keyTakeaway: Low or negative correlations between portfolio assets improve diversification. In crises, correlations tend to spike toward +1.

name: Maximum Drawdown
aliases: max drawdown, drawdown, peak to trough
definition: The largest peak-to-trough decline in portfolio value before a new peak is reached. Measures worst-case scenario historically.
keyTakeaway: S&P 500 max drawdowns: 2008 (-57%), 2020 (-34%), 2022 (-25%). Know your pain tolerance before investing aggressively.

name: IPO
aliases: initial public offering, going public, ipo investing
definition: The first time a private company offers shares to the public on a stock exchange. Transforms a private company into a publicly traded one.
keyTakeaway: IPOs often pop on day one but underperform over 3-5 years. Most retail investors can't access IPO pricing — wait for the lockup period to expire.

name: Dark Pool
aliases: dark pools, alternative trading system, ats
definition: Private exchanges where large institutional orders are matched anonymously to minimize market impact.
keyTakeaway: Dark pools account for ~40% of US equity volume. They provide anonymity but reduce price transparency for retail investors.

name: Market Maker
aliases: market making, designated market maker, dmm, liquidity provider
definition: A firm that continuously provides buy and sell quotes for a security, profiting from the bid-ask spread while providing liquidity.
keyTakeaway: Market makers (like Citadel Securities, Virtu) facilitate trading and narrow spreads. They take on inventory risk in exchange for the spread.

name: Circuit Breaker
aliases: market halt, trading halt, limit up limit down
definition: Mechanisms that temporarily halt trading when prices drop too fast. S&P 500 triggers: Level 1 (-7%), Level 2 (-13%), Level 3 (-20%).
keyTakeaway: Circuit breakers prevent panic selling. They were triggered in March 2020 multiple times during the COVID crash.

name: Market Order vs Limit Order
aliases: market order, limit order, stop order, order types
definition: Market orders execute immediately at the current price. Limit orders execute only at a specified price or better.
keyTakeaway: Always use limit orders for illiquid stocks or large trades. Market orders on volatile stocks can result in significant slippage.

name: Bid-Ask Spread
aliases: bid ask, spread, bid price, ask price
definition: The difference between the highest price a buyer will pay (bid) and the lowest price a seller will accept (ask).
keyTakeaway: Narrower spreads indicate higher liquidity. Wide spreads on small-cap or penny stocks increase trading costs significantly.

name: Emergency Fund
aliases: rainy day fund, emergency savings, cash reserve
definition: Cash savings to cover unexpected expenses or income loss. Standard recommendation: 3-6 months of essential expenses.
keyTakeaway: Keep in a High-Yield Savings Account (4-5% APY). Visa holders (H1B) should target 12-18 months due to employment uncertainty.

name: Budgeting
aliases: budget, 50/30/20 rule, monthly budget, cash flow
definition: A plan for managing income and expenses. Popular frameworks include: 50/30/20 (needs/wants/savings) and zero-based budgeting.
keyTakeaway: Track spending for 1-2 months to understand patterns. Automate savings with 'pay yourself first' transfers on payday.

name: Credit Score
aliases: fico score, credit rating, credit report, credit history
definition: A numerical rating (300-850) representing creditworthiness. Based on payment history (35%), amounts owed (30%), length (15%), new credit (10%), mix (10%).
keyTakeaway: 740+ = excellent rates on mortgages. Never miss payments. Keep credit utilization below 30% (ideally <10%).

name: Debt Snowball vs Avalanche
aliases: debt payoff, debt snowball, debt avalanche, debt repayment
definition: Snowball: pay off smallest debts first for psychological wins. Avalanche: pay off highest-interest debts first for mathematical optimization.
keyTakeaway: Avalanche saves the most money mathematically. Snowball provides motivation through quick wins. Both beat minimum payments.

name: Net Worth
aliases: total net worth, assets minus liabilities, wealth
definition: Total assets minus total liabilities. A comprehensive measure of financial health that goes beyond income.
keyTakeaway: Track net worth monthly. A positive and growing net worth is more important than high income. Include all assets and debts.

name: High-Yield Savings Account
aliases: hysa, high yield savings, online savings account
definition: Online savings accounts offering 4-5% APY (vs 0.01-0.5% at traditional banks). FDIC insured up to $250,000.
keyTakeaway: Perfect for emergency funds and short-term goals. Popular options: Marcus, Ally, Wealthfront, SoFi. Rates fluctuate with the Fed Funds rate.

name: Mortgage
aliases: home loan, mortgage rate, fixed rate mortgage, adjustable rate mortgage
definition: A loan secured by real property. Types: fixed-rate (stable payments) or adjustable-rate (initially lower, can change). Standard term: 30 or 15 years.
keyTakeaway: 20% down payment avoids PMI ($100s/month). 15-year mortgages save massive interest but have higher monthly payments.

name: Down Payment
aliases: home down payment, 20 percent down, pmi
definition: Upfront cash payment when purchasing a home. Standard is 20% to avoid Private Mortgage Insurance (PMI).
keyTakeaway: PMI typically costs 0.5-1% of the loan annually. FHA loans allow 3.5% down but include MIP. VA loans require 0% down.

name: Dollar Weighted Return
aliases: money weighted return, irr, internal rate of return, personal return
definition: The return that accounts for the timing and size of all cash flows into and out of an investment.
keyTakeaway: Dollar-weighted return reflects YOUR actual experience. It differs from time-weighted return when you add/withdraw money at different times.

name: Price-to-Book Ratio
aliases: p/b ratio, price to book, book value
definition: Stock Price ÷ Book Value Per Share. Compares market value to accounting value of a company.
keyTakeaway: P/B < 1.0 may signal undervaluation (market values company below its assets). Banks and financial stocks commonly use P/B.

name: Free Cash Flow
aliases: fcf, free cash flow yield, cash flow from operations
definition: Operating cash flow minus capital expenditures. Represents cash available for dividends, buybacks, or debt repayment.
keyTakeaway: Companies with strong, positive FCF have more financial flexibility. FCF is harder to manipulate than earnings.

name: Debt-to-Equity Ratio
aliases: d/e ratio, leverage ratio, financial leverage
definition: Total Debt ÷ Total Shareholders' Equity. Measures how much a company relies on debt vs equity financing.
keyTakeaway: Higher D/E = more financial risk. D/E > 2.0 is generally considered high. Varies significantly by industry (utilities vs tech).

name: Return on Equity
aliases: roe, return on equity formula, shareholder returns
definition: Net Income ÷ Shareholders' Equity. Measures how effectively a company uses shareholder capital to generate profit.
keyTakeaway: ROE > 15% is generally considered good. Very high ROE (>30%) may indicate high leverage. Compare within the same industry.

name: Expense Ratio
aliases: fund expense ratio, management fee, ter, total expense ratio
definition: Annual fee charged by mutual funds or ETFs as a percentage of assets. Covers management, administration, and operating costs.
keyTakeaway: A 1% expense ratio costs ~$28K on a $100K investment over 20 years (at 7% return). Choose funds with 0.03-0.20% ratios when possible.

name: Rebalancing
aliases: portfolio rebalancing, asset reallocation, drift
definition: Periodically adjusting portfolio holdings back to target allocations. Sell winners, buy losers to maintain desired risk level.
keyTakeaway: Rebalance annually or when allocations drift >5% from targets. Tax-efficient method: direct new contributions to underweight assets.

name: Value Investing
aliases: value investor, graham, buffett, intrinsic value, margin of safety
definition: Buying stocks trading below their intrinsic value. Pioneered by Benjamin Graham, popularized by Warren Buffett.
keyTakeaway: Look for low P/E, low P/B, strong cash flow, and a margin of safety. Value has historically outperformed growth over long periods.

name: Growth Investing
aliases: growth investor, growth stocks, momentum investing
definition: Investing in companies expected to grow revenue and earnings faster than the market average, even if current valuations are high.
keyTakeaway: Growth stocks can deliver outsized returns but are more volatile. They often have high P/E ratios and may not pay dividends.

name: FBAR
aliases: foreign bank account report, fincen 114, foreign account reporting
definition: US persons with foreign financial accounts exceeding $10,000 at any time during the year must file FinCEN Form 114 by April 15.
keyTakeaway: Failure to file FBAR can result in penalties up to $100K per violation. Covers NRE/NRO accounts, foreign bank accounts, mutual funds.

name: NRE Account
aliases: non-resident external account, nre fd, nre savings
definition: Indian bank account for NRIs to deposit foreign earnings. Fully repatriable (can transfer back to US). Interest is tax-free in India.
keyTakeaway: NRE FDs offer 6-7% interest (tax-free in India, taxable in US). Must be reported on FBAR if balance exceeds $10,000.

name: NRO Account
aliases: non-resident ordinary account, nro savings, india income account
definition: Indian bank account for NRIs to manage India-sourced income (rent, dividends). Limited repatriation ($1M/year after tax). Interest taxed at 30% in India.
keyTakeaway: Use NRO for rental income from Indian property. Interest income is taxable in both India and US (use DTAA for credit).

name: DTAA
aliases: double taxation avoidance agreement, tax treaty, us india tax treaty
definition: Treaty between countries to prevent the same income from being taxed in both. US-India DTAA covers dividends, interest, capital gains, and services.
keyTakeaway: Claim Foreign Tax Credit (Form 1116) on US returns for taxes paid in India. DTAA prevents double taxation on NRO interest, rental income.

name: FATCA
aliases: foreign account tax compliance act, form 8938
definition: US law requiring foreign financial institutions to report US account holders. US persons must file Form 8938 for foreign assets exceeding thresholds.
keyTakeaway: Form 8938 thresholds: $50K (single, US) or $200K (single, abroad). FATCA and FBAR are separate filings with different thresholds.

name: PFIC
aliases: passive foreign investment company, pfic tax, foreign mutual fund
definition: A foreign corporation where 75%+ income is passive or 50%+ assets produce passive income. Subject to punitive US tax treatment.
keyTakeaway: Indian mutual funds are classified as PFICs. This makes them extremely tax-inefficient for US persons — avoid holding them.

name: H1B Visa Financial Planning
aliases: h1b, h1b visa, work visa, immigration financial planning
definition: Financial considerations for H1B visa holders: limited job mobility, 60-day grace period if terminated, green card backlog, dual-country tax obligations.
keyTakeaway: Build 12-18 month emergency fund (vs 3-6 months standard). Maintain India relocation reserve. Max out 401K early — it's portable.

name: Stock Split
aliases: forward split, reverse split, stock split history
definition: A corporate action that increases (forward split) or decreases (reverse split) shares outstanding while maintaining the same total market value.
keyTakeaway: A 4-for-1 split turns 1 share at $400 into 4 shares at $100. Doesn't change value but improves accessibility and often increases trading volume.

name: Stock Buyback
aliases: share repurchase, buyback program, treasury stock
definition: When a company purchases its own shares from the market, reducing shares outstanding and increasing EPS for remaining shareholders.
keyTakeaway: Buybacks are more tax-efficient than dividends for shareholders. However, they don't always signal strength — some companies buy back at inflated prices.

name: Dividend Reinvestment Plan
aliases: drip, drip investing, automatic reinvestment
definition: Automatically reinvesting dividends to purchase additional shares of the same stock or fund, often commission-free.
keyTakeaway: DRIPs harness compounding by continuously increasing your share count. Over 20+ years, reinvested dividends can account for 40%+ of total returns.

name: Modern Portfolio Theory
aliases: mpt, efficient frontier, markowitz, mean-variance optimization
definition: Framework for constructing portfolios that maximize expected return for a given level of risk, using asset correlations and expected returns.
keyTakeaway: The 'efficient frontier' shows the best possible return for each risk level. Diversification across uncorrelated assets improves the frontier.

name: Monte Carlo Simulation
aliases: monte carlo, probability simulation, retirement simulation
definition: A computerized mathematical technique that uses random sampling to model the probability of different outcomes in financial planning.
keyTakeaway: Monte Carlo shows the range of possible outcomes (e.g., 85% chance of meeting your retirement goal). A 95%+ success rate is generally targeted.

name: Safe Withdrawal Rate
aliases: 4% rule, swr, trinity study, retirement withdrawal
definition: The percentage of a retirement portfolio that can be withdrawn annually with low risk of depletion over 30 years. The '4% rule' comes from the Trinity Study.
keyTakeaway: 4% is a starting point, not a guarantee. Adjust based on market conditions, spending flexibility, and retirement length. Some suggest 3.5% for safety.

name: Asset Location
aliases: asset location strategy, tax-efficient placement, account type optimization
definition: Placing investments in the most tax-efficient account type: bonds in tax-deferred (401K/IRA), stocks in taxable/Roth for lower tax burden.
keyTakeaway: Put high-growth assets in Roth (tax-free growth), bonds/REITs in Traditional IRA (defer ordinary income), and index funds in taxable (low turnover).

name: Backdoor Roth IRA
aliases: backdoor roth, mega backdoor roth, roth conversion strategy
definition: A strategy for high-income earners to contribute to a Roth IRA by first contributing to a Traditional IRA, then converting. Bypasses income limits.
keyTakeaway: Works best with no existing Traditional IRA balance (avoids pro-rata rule). Mega backdoor Roth uses after-tax 401(k) contributions — up to $69K total.

name: FIRE Movement
aliases: financial independence retire early, fire, lean fire, fat fire, coast fire
definition: A lifestyle movement focused on extreme savings (50-70% of income) and investment to achieve financial independence and optional early retirement.
keyTakeaway: FI number = annual expenses × 25 (based on 4% rule). Lean FIRE: <$40K/yr expenses. Fat FIRE: >$100K/yr. Coast FIRE: enough invested to stop saving.

name: Dollar Index
aliases: dxy, us dollar index, dollar strength
definition: Measures the US dollar's value against a basket of 6 major currencies (EUR, JPY, GBP, CAD, SEK, CHF). Base value: 100 (1973).
keyTakeaway: A strong dollar hurts US exporters and multinational earnings but benefits importers. DXY above 100 = dollar stronger than 1973 baseline.

name: Yield Curve
aliases: yield curve inversion, inverted yield curve, treasury yield curve
definition: A graph showing interest rates on US Treasury bonds across different maturities. Normal: upward sloping. Inverted: short-term > long-term.
keyTakeaway: An inverted yield curve has preceded every US recession since 1955. It signals that investors expect lower rates (and slower growth) in the future.

name: Federal Reserve
aliases: the fed, federal funds rate, fomc, monetary policy, interest rate decision
definition: The central bank of the United States. Sets monetary policy through the Federal Funds Rate, quantitative easing/tightening, and bank regulation.
keyTakeaway: Rate hikes slow the economy and hurt stocks (especially growth). Rate cuts stimulate growth and benefit stocks. Watch FOMC meetings (8x/year).

name: Inflation
aliases: cpi, consumer price index, inflation rate, purchasing power
definition: The rate at which general prices increase over time, eroding purchasing power. Measured by the Consumer Price Index (CPI).
keyTakeaway: The Fed targets 2% inflation. Investments must earn more than inflation to grow in real terms. TIPS bonds and I-Bonds protect against inflation.

name: TIPS
aliases: treasury inflation protected securities, tips bonds, inflation bonds, i bonds
definition: US Treasury bonds whose principal adjusts with inflation (CPI). Protects purchasing power while providing guaranteed real returns.
keyTakeaway: I-Bonds ($10K/year limit) offer inflation protection with no downside risk. TIPS trade on the open market and can fluctuate in price.

name: Sector Rotation
aliases: sector rotation strategy, business cycle sectors, cyclical vs defensive
definition: The strategy of moving investments between market sectors based on the economic cycle. Different sectors outperform at different stages.
keyTakeaway: Early recovery: Technology, Consumer Discretionary. Mid-cycle: Industrials, Materials. Late cycle: Energy, Staples. Recession: Utilities, Healthcare.

name: ESG Investing
aliases: esg, sustainable investing, socially responsible investing, sri
definition: Investing based on Environmental, Social, and Governance criteria alongside financial returns. Screens out companies with poor ESG practices.
keyTakeaway: ESG funds have shown competitive returns vs traditional funds. However, ESG ratings vary across providers and there's a risk of greenwashing.

name: Fibonacci Retracement
aliases: fib levels, fibonacci, 61.8%, golden ratio finance
definition: Technical analysis tool using horizontal lines at key Fibonacci percentages (23.6%, 38.2%, 50%, 61.8%) to identify potential support/resistance levels.
keyTakeaway: The 61.8% level is the most watched. While not scientifically proven, Fibonacci levels become self-fulfilling prophecies because many traders use them.

name: MACD
aliases: moving average convergence divergence, macd indicator, macd crossover
definition: A momentum indicator showing the relationship between two moving averages. MACD line crossing above signal line = bullish; below = bearish.
keyTakeaway: MACD works best in trending markets. Combine with RSI and volume for stronger signals. Divergence between MACD and price often signals reversals.

name: Bollinger Bands
aliases: bollinger, bollinger band squeeze, band width
definition: Technical indicator with a middle band (20-day SMA) and upper/lower bands set 2 standard deviations away. Measures volatility and potential reversals.
keyTakeaway: Price touching the upper band doesn't mean 'sell' — in strong uptrends, price rides the upper band. A 'squeeze' (narrow bands) often precedes a big move.

name: Volume
aliases: trading volume, volume analysis, volume profile, obv
definition: The number of shares or contracts traded in a security during a given period. Confirms the strength of price movements.
keyTakeaway: Rising prices on rising volume = strong trend. Rising prices on declining volume = weakening trend. Volume spikes often occur at trend reversals.

name: Support and Resistance
aliases: support level, resistance level, price floor, price ceiling
definition: Price levels where a stock tends to stop falling (support) or stop rising (resistance). Created by historical buying/selling patterns.
keyTakeaway: When broken, support becomes resistance and vice versa. The more times a level is tested, the more significant the eventual breakout.

name: Estate Planning
aliases: will, trust, estate tax, inheritance, beneficiary designation
definition: The process of arranging how your assets will be managed and distributed after death. Includes wills, trusts, beneficiary designations, and powers of attorney.
keyTakeaway: Beneficiary designations on retirement accounts and insurance override your will. Review and update after major life events (marriage, kids, divorce).

name: Life Insurance
aliases: term life, whole life, universal life, life insurance policy
definition: Financial protection for dependents. Term life: coverage for a set period, cheapest. Whole life: lifetime coverage + cash value, much more expensive.
keyTakeaway: Most people need term life insurance (10-20x annual income, 20-30 year term). Whole life is rarely the optimal choice — invest the premium difference instead.

name: Umbrella Insurance
aliases: excess liability, umbrella policy, personal liability
definition: Extra liability coverage beyond auto and homeowner policies. Typically $1-5M in coverage for $200-500/year.
keyTakeaway: Essential if your net worth exceeds your auto/home liability limits. Protects against lawsuits, personal liability, and catastrophic claims.

name: Index Rebalancing
aliases: index reconstitution, nasdaq 100 changes, sp500 additions, russell reconstitution
definition: The periodic process of adding and removing stocks from market indices. Creates massive passive fund flows as index-tracking ETFs must adjust holdings.
keyTakeaway: Stocks added to S&P 500 see $16T+ in passive flows. Front-running anticipated additions before announcement can generate 3-7% alpha.
